title: South Africa Financial Update | Key Market & Economic Developments | 23 July 2026
summary: SARB holds the repo rate at 7.00% in a 4-2 vote as 2026 GDP growth is forecast at 1.2%, while ARM unveils a R16bn capex plan, Pepkor eyes a R21.3bn fintech spin-off, and Mozambique's looming 2028 gas cliff threatens industrial energy costs.
body:
The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) has kept the repo rate unchanged at 7.00%, leaving the prime lending rate at 10.50%. The Monetary Policy Committee's decision was split 4–2, with four members opting to hold rates steady while two supported a 25-basis-point increase. Although headline inflation edged up to 5.0% in June, driven by fuel price volatility and persistent services inflation, easing global oil prices and a resilient rand provided enough support for the Bank to pause further tightening. Economic growth expectations remain modest, with GDP forecast at around 1.2% for 2026, reflecting high operating costs and subdued consumer and business confidence.

On the corporate front, African Rainbow Minerals (ARM) came under pressure after unveiling a nearly R16 billion capital investment plan focused on expanding its mining operations. Pepkor announced a significant R21.3 billion fintech expansion strategy, with long-term ambitions of listing the business separately as it strengthens its presence in South Africa's informal economy. Meanwhile, Absa is nearing a strategic transaction in Tanzania that would consolidate approximately R49.2 billion in regional banking assets. In retail, Mr Price reported encouraging quarterly sales growth, supported by continued strong demand across its NKD business.

Attention is also turning to longer-term structural challenges. Industry stakeholders continue to warn about South Africa's looming 2028 gas cliff, as natural gas supplies from Mozambique's Pande and Temane fields are expected to decline significantly after 2028. Without accelerated investment in LNG infrastructure, many industrial users could face energy costs between two and five times higher if forced to switch to LPG or diesel alternatives. At the same time, the technology sector is responding to a recent cyberattack that prompted Mustek and Rectron to launch forensic recovery efforts, while Treasury continues to defend funding freezes affecting 69 financially distressed municipalities, highlighting ongoing concerns over municipal finances and their impact on the broader business environment.
author: Business News Hub Desk
